guest_role: Founding Managing Partner at Pear VC
assertiveness_why: Harry asks warm, admiring, mostly chronological biographical questions with zero pushback or contested claims throughout.
question_style: Almost entirely open-ended, narrative-prompting questions ('talk to me about how you arrived...', 'what was behind that decision?') that invite storytelling; Harry never challenges a claim, plants no competing thesis, and does not interrupt.
tone: Extremely warm, fan-like and reverent ('one of the kindest, most genuine... guests I've had'), high energy, some light self-deprecating banter about Chelsea vs Man United.
depth: Surface-to-narrative depth focused on biography and relationship-building philosophy rather than technical or strategic operating detail; light follow-up on fund mechanics (prorata, dilution in Danger) and fund structure/naming.
harry_quotes: What perspective did he give you when you saw him?
Was that a big learning curve for you?
So what was behind that decision?
learnings: Nozad built his entire deal flow and reputation through in-person relationship building rather than pattern-matching resumes or decks.
Losing prorata rights on Danger due to lack of follow-on capital taught him that maintaining ownership through reserves is critical to fund economics.
He evaluates founders by spending unstructured time with them outside the office (e.g., long dinners) to assess character, not just the product.
Founding teams with pre-existing personal history and shared struggle tend to build enduring companies.
He deliberately built an affinity network (Persian-American founders and students) as a distinct sourcing edge.
notable: Guest's origin story (undocumented immigrant, rug salesman, early Dropbox/Danger investor) is unusually extraordinary and dominates the episode over any investing-craft analysis.
